title: Dot with Counts
subtitle: {n}{key:.} — replay the change with a new count.
summary: Pressing a count before . replaces the count baked into the original change. So 5dw then 10. deletes 10 words, not 5.

blocks.text: The dot register stores the change *with* its count. When you press {n}{key:.}, the new count *replaces* the original. This is rarely the first thing people learn but it's a force multiplier.
Example: {key:d}{key:w} deletes a word. {key:5}{key:.} deletes 5 words at the new cursor position. The original count (1, implicit) was discarded; 5 took its place.

blocks.title: How the Count Substitution Works
blocks.text:
Internally the dot register holds the change as a sequence: optional count, optional register, the operator, the motion (with its own optional count), and any inserted text.

When you press {key:.}, Vim replays the recorded sequence verbatim. When you press {n}{key:.}, the prefix you just typed becomes the *outer* count for the replay — the operator-side count, not the motion-side count. The motion's own internal count is preserved.

So if your original change was {key:d}{key:3}{key:w} (delete 3 words), {key:.} repeats it as 3 words; {key:5}{key:.} repeats it as 5 × 3 = 15 words. Counts multiply, just like in [`counts.counts`](#counts.counts).
{key:0}{key:.} is meaningless (counts can't be 0). Just use {key:.} for "once."

rows: Each dot can have its own count — they don't accumulate

blocks.title: Dot's count overrides the original
blocks.text: Prefix {key:.} with a count and the replay uses your count, not the count baked into the recorded change. Internally the struct stores the original count separately; at replay time, the executor swaps in any new count provided. So `5.` after a single-line change deletes five lines, not one — the change template is reused with a new multiplier.
